run.output: Query executed successfully, returned 10 rows in 3594 ms

validation.comparison_result: 3 row(s) differ - expected vs actual revenue and dates completely wrong

Q3.notes: Results are WRONG. Comparison tool found 3 row mismatches. Revenue values are larger than expected (524569 vs 440715), dates are all 1994-01-01 or 1995-01-01 instead of specific Feb/Mar 1995 dates.

Q3.bottleneck: MULTIPLE CRITICAL CORRECTNESS ISSUES - semantic errors in date handling and filtering
Q3.root_cause: Code has three fundamental semantic bugs: (1) Dates stored as epoch days (int32_t, 9000+ range for 1990s), but code treats them as year-only integers (1992-1998 range). (2) Date constants (orderdate_cutoff=1996, shipdate_cutoff=1994) are year integers, not epoch days. Should be ~9204 and ~9131 respectively. (3) dateFormatToString() outputs hardcoded Jan 01 for all dates instead of converting epoch days to actual YYYY-MM-DD format. (4) Filter logic with year-only cutoffs allows wrong rows through, inflating revenues with data from wrong time periods.

analysis.overall: CRITICAL CORRECTNESS FAILURE. The Q3 query produces completely wrong results. Date handling is fundamentally broken - dates are stored as epoch days but code interprets them as years. Filters use year integers (1996, 1994) instead of epoch days. Output format shows '1994-01-01' and '1995-01-01' for all results instead of specific Feb/Mar 1995 dates. Revenue calculation is wrong due to improper filtering. Query must be fixed before any performance optimizations can be done. Hardware: 64 CPUs (32 cores HT), L3 44MB, HDD - parallelism is not currently the bottleneck since results are incorrect.

critical_fixes.issue: Date columns contain epoch days (int32_t, days since 1970), but code treats them as year-only integers
critical_fixes.fix: Replace year-based interpretation with proper epoch-day conversion. Implement correct dateToEpochDays() function or use reference implementation from knowledge base.
critical_fixes.description: Lines 118-126: Change logic from year-only comparisons to epoch day comparisons. Reference dates: 1995-03-15 = 9204 epoch days (not 1995 or 1996). 1995-03-15 also = 9204. For o_orderdate < 1995-03-15: use epoch_days < 9204. For l_shipdate > 1995-03-15: use epoch_days > 9204. Remove lines 125-126 (wrong year-only cutoffs). Replace with correct epoch day constants calculated via proper date conversion.

critical_fixes.issue: dateFormatToString() outputs hardcoded '1994-01-01' format instead of converting epoch days to actual dates
critical_fixes.fix: Implement proper epoch-day to YYYY-MM-DD conversion function
critical_fixes.description: Lines 36-42: Current function snprintf-outputs year as first parameter then '-01-01'. This is wrong. Must convert int32_t epoch days to actual year/month/day. Algorithm: (a) Calculate year from days, (b) subtract year's day count, (c) find month from remaining days, (d) calculate day. Use standard calendrical algorithm or reference from storage/encoding-handling.md knowledge base.

critical_fixes.issue: Filter predicates use wrong date values - comparing epoch day columns to year integers
critical_fixes.fix: Update date comparison logic to use correct epoch day constants
critical_fixes.description: Line 168 (o_orderdate[i] < orderdate_cutoff): orderdate_cutoff=1996 is wrong. Dates are epoch days, so comparing to 1996 means 'all rows with epoch day < 1996', which is most data from 1970-1975. Should compare to 9204 (1995-03-15). Line 180 (l_shipdate[i] > shipdate_cutoff): shipdate_cutoff=1994 is wrong. Should be 9204 (1995-03-15). The SQL requires o_orderdate < '1995-03-15' AND l_shipdate > '1995-03-15' - these should be expressed as epoch day comparisons where 1995-03-15 ≈ 9204 days.

performance_optimizations.description: After correctness is fixed: Current code uses single thread for all three scans (customer, orders, lineitem). Hardware: 64 CPUs (32 cores + HT). Implement morsel-driven parallelism per knowledge base (parallelism/thread-parallelism.md). Split customer scan into ~64 chunks of ~23K rows each, process in parallel with thread-local hash maps. Same for orders and lineitem. Use std::vector<std::thread> with thread pool or std::async. Merge thread-local results at end. Expected speedup: 8-15x on multi-socket system (actual may be limited by L3 contention on 2-socket 44MB L3).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 9.3x speedup (from 3594ms to ~390ms target). Current bottleneck is fully single-threaded processing of 75M rows across three tables. Each scan could run on 32 cores = ~30-32x theoretical max, but realistic target given cache contention and merge overhead is ~8-15x.

performance_optimizations.description: Current code mmap-loads columns with MADV_SEQUENTIAL (sequential access hint). Optimize: (1) Only load needed columns (c_custkey, c_mktsegment for customer; o_orderkey, o_custkey, o_orderdate, o_shippriority for orders; l_orderkey, l_extendedprice, l_discount, l_shipdate for lineitem). Avoid loading unused columns (c_name, c_address, c_comment, etc.). (2) Add MADV_WILLNEED before loop to prefetch. (3) Consider zone map filtering on l_shipdate to skip blocks with date ranges outside 1995-03-15. Storage design shows 'idx_lineitem_shipdate' zone map available.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 10-20% reduction in I/O via column pruning (load ~30% of lineitem columns). Zone map filtering unlikely to help much since date range after filter is ~50% of data, but saves index traversal cost (~5-10%).

performance_optimizations.technique: Join Algorithm Optimization - Partitioned Hash Join or Sort-Merge
performance_optimizations.description: Current code uses two sequential hash joins: (1) customer_map (300K entries) + orders scan → orders_map (7.3M entries), (2) orders_map probe + lineitem scan. Hash table lookups dominate. Optimize: (1) Partitioning: Split lineitem into 8 partitions by l_orderkey % 8, join each partition independently to fit in L3 cache (44MB). (2) Or use sort-merge join on pre-sorted keys (lineitem sorted by l_orderkey per storage design). (3) Reorder joins: probe lineitem first (60M rows, ~50% selectivity after date filter = 30M), then join to orders (7.3M) rather than building large orders_map upfront.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 20-30% reduction in join phase via better cache utilization. Partitioned join fits working set in L3, reduces cache misses from orders_map lookups.

performance_optimizations.description: Current code uses single global std::map for aggregation (GROUP BY l_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_shippriority). With ~100K distinct groups, this map grows large and causes memory bandwidth saturation. Implement partial aggregation: (1) Thread-local pre-aggregation during lineitem scan (each thread maintains local map), (2) After filtering, merge local maps into global map. Reduces global map contention and improves cache locality. Alternatively, use compact hash table (Robin Hood or Swiss table from data-structures/compact-hash-tables.md) instead of std::map.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 15-25% via reduced lock contention and better cache behavior. Map operations are O(log n) for std::map; switching to hash table and pre-agg gives ~2-3x speedup on aggregation phase.

summary: QUERY Q3 IS BROKEN - Must fix critical semantic errors before optimization. (1) Date handling is fundamentally wrong: code interprets epoch days (9000+ range) as year-only integers (1992-1998 range). (2) Filter cutoffs use year integers (1996, 1994) instead of epoch days (~9204, ~9131). (3) Output formatting is hardcoded '1994-01-01' instead of converting epoch days. These errors cause wrong results and must be fixed immediately. After correctness is restored, performance bottlenecks are clear: (1) Single-threaded execution on 64-core system (9.3x gap to benchmark), (2) Hash table lookups for large orders_map (7.3M entries), (3) Aggregation into large map (100K groups). Recommended approach: (1) CRITICAL: Fix date handling per semantic fixes above, (2) Add thread parallelism to utilize 32 cores effectively (expected ~8-15x speedup to reach ~390ms), (3) Optimize joins and aggregation structure for cache efficiency.
